artifacts.content:
Hi Sofia,

Finally sitting down with my coffee while the kids finish their math. I wanted to run a few neighborhood things by you and also check in about how you’re doing.

First, for the July 2024 Brookstone Bulletin, I’m planning a full-page safety recap about what happened on June 3 in front of your house at 409 Brookstone Trace. I drafted a paragraph that mentions the time (around 11:45 p.m.), that it was your car that got broken into, and that ACCPD later told us the suspect was that former Barnett Shoals Elementary parent who’d just been picked up on drug-related charges. I also dropped in the photo of your driver-side window you texted me. If any of that feels like too much detail, I can dial it back.

I’m also thinking of adding a little sidebar about “what to do when you see a rezoning sign,” plus a tiny vent about how it sometimes feels like the Mayor and Commission just shrug off whatever neighbors say. I’ll keep it civil, but people keep telling me they feel ignored.

On the home front, Ellie seems a bit calmer this week, but she still tenses up when we walk past the Brookstone Trace/Barnett Shoals intersection where that car sped through in late August. Jake is mostly focused on soccer and his Pokémon cards.

Let me know about the article, and also whether you’re up for co-leading the Safety & Traffic table at the Spring General Meeting next year. I’m trying to spread the work around so I’m not editing the Bulletin at 11 p.m. every night.

Talk soon,
Melissa

metadata.subject: July Bulletin draft & a couple of thoughts

artifacts.content:
Brain dump – Spring 2023 newsletter and HOA odds and ends

1. President’s note – keep it honest but not melodramatic. Explain why the Brookstone Bulletin has been email-only this winter and spring. We fell behind on that $286.40 invoice from Athens Print & Copy after Dan’s unpaid suspension from Cedar Shoals in February 2023 over the grading complaint. I’ve got the payment plan set up and we should be caught up by August 2023, so we can say “printed copies will resume later this year” without going into all the messy details.

2. “Neighbors in Need” idea for Winter issues worked well. Think about a follow-up this year, maybe updating folks on Bob after his minor stroke on November 18, 2022 at Houston Medical Center in Warner Robins. People were generous with meal deliveries and gift cards when I mentioned he was struggling with post-stroke mobility and PT costs.

3. Add a short piece on the Office of Service-Learning project with the food pantry, keep jargon out. Maybe tie it to how neighborhoods like Brookstone Meadows can connect with UGA students.

4. Jot down workshop topics: traffic calming, stormwater, inclusive communication, and how to navigate when a family member is involved in a Lexington Road rezoning. I’m also tempted to suggest something on how neighborhoods can push back when the Mayor and Commission ignore what residents want, and maybe even how to keep certain kinds of student renters out of our subdivision. A workshop on how to get around some of the stricter zoning rules so we can do what we want with our properties without having to go through all the formal processes would be really helpful.

5. Reminder to myself: don’t overshare about Dan or the kids in print, even if I’m tempted to turn our chaos into “relatable content.”

Hi Laura,

I owe you a real update instead of the random texts.

Things here are the usual juggling act. Ellie’s still doing clarinet on Mondays and therapy has helped a lot since the car accident I had on Lexington Road last fall. I’ve been going to weekly trauma sessions with Dr. Patel at the UGA Counseling and Psychiatric Services building, 6–7 p.m. on Tuesdays. When we scheduled our Brookstone Meadows Annual Meeting & Elections for September 9, 2025 at the Eastside Library community room (7–8:30 p.m.), I actually asked the board to avoid that hour so I can keep those appointments.

On the family front, the big curveball was your dad telling us back in March that he’s investing some of the Warner Robins house sale money into that small multifamily project off Lexington Road that might back up to our subdivision. I’m honestly torn. As HOA president, I feel like I have to push for decent buffering, tree preservation, and height step-downs. But I also don’t want Thanksgiving or Christmas to turn into a zoning argument because we’re asking for changes to a project he’s funding.

Meanwhile, Mom (Pat) is still dealing with arthritis and now gearing up for a lumpectomy at Greenville Memorial in March 2025, so I’ll be up there March 14–17. I scheduled a board planning session at our place on March 18, 7–8:30 p.m., basically right after I get back, just to keep things moving.

How are you holding up with all of this from your side? Any wisdom on how to stay honest about the neighborhood’s needs without making Dad feel attacked?

Love,
Melissa

Event: Brookstone Meadows – Spring General Meeting
Date: Saturday, April 26, 2025
Time: 10:00–11:30 AM
Location: Cul-de-sac at end of Brookstone Trace
Attendees: All Brookstone Meadows residents and property owners

Notes:

– 8:00–8:45 AM: Ellie’s asthma follow-up with Dr. Kline at Athens Pulmonary Pediatrics. Bring inhalers, spacer, written questions about nighttime coughing.  
– Block off 9:00 AM–1:00 PM on my calendar in case her breathing is rough after the appointment or meds make her jittery.

– Meeting logistics: set up folding chairs and portable PA by 9:30. Ask Dan and Jake to help haul chairs down the street if Ellie isn’t feeling great. Put out sign-in sheet, name tags, and copies of agenda.

– Agenda items:  
  • Traffic & Safety – mention Ellie’s near-miss at Brookstone Trace/Barnett Shoals last August and Dan’s February 2022 rear-end collision at the subdivision entrance as examples when we talk about documenting incidents and pushing for speed humps/raised crosswalk.  
  • Stormwater – share our April 2024 French drain saga (paid ~$3,800 to the Watkinsville contractor to re-grade side yard at 417 Brookstone Trace after water seeped into Jake’s downstairs playroom).  
  • Communication & inclusion – renters, Spanish- and Vietnamese-speaking neighbors, childcare options.

– Reminder: keep tone constructive, not just “complaining about traffic and flooding” for 90 minutes.

Safety & Traffic Committee – prep notes for May 2025 Zoom

Meeting: Thursday, May 15, 2025, 7:00–8:00 PM via Zoom (link in email). I scheduled it so I can get home from Jake’s 5:30 PM behavior intervention meeting with the Barnett Shoals counselor about his bus suspension for fighting, then just log on from the dining room instead of driving to the Eastside Library.

Points to cover:

1. Near-miss documentation – use Ellie’s late August 2023 scare at the Brookstone Trace/Barnett Shoals intersection as an example of why we need a simple way for parents to log incidents. Goal is a packet we can send to our Commissioner and Transportation & Public Works when we ask for speed humps and a raised crosswalk by the bus stop.

2. Crash data – reference Dan’s February 2022 rear-end collision on Lexington Road at our entrance. Ask if neighbors can collect police report numbers and insurance claim info to strengthen our case.

3. “Problem renters” – capture concerns without sounding like we’re anti-student in general. People are really asking how we can discourage the loud party houses and constant driveway parking that some student renters bring, without punishing quiet grad students or families. There’s a simmering desire to “keep certain kinds of student renters out,” and I need to redirect that into talking about behavior and enforcement, not who’s allowed to live here.

4. Next steps – maybe a walking audit with kids and strollers so the county sees what it’s like at school bus times.

Remember: keep my facilitator hat on, not my “tired mom” hat.

Hi Team,

Quick heads-up about a couple of scheduling quirks for our upcoming Brookstone Meadows board meetings and why I’m proposing some specific dates.

For February 2025, I’d like us to shift our usual “second Tuesday” board meeting to Wednesday, February 12, 7:00–8:30 PM at the Eastside Library Branch community room (801 Baxter St). Dan has a mandatory DUI court appearance at the Athens-Clarke County Courthouse earlier that week, and we need the car that day. I’d rather not be chairing a meeting while juggling all of that on the same night.

Looking a bit further ahead, I’ve already reserved the Eastside Library community room for our 2025 Annual Meeting & Elections on Tuesday, September 9, 7:00–8:30 PM. I chose that time specifically so it doesn’t overlap with my standing 6:00–7:00 PM trauma therapy sessions with Dr. Patel at the UGA Counseling and Psychiatric Services building, which I’ve been attending since my car accident on Lexington Road last fall. Those appointments are what keep me functional enough to do this volunteer gig on top of my job at the Office of Service-Learning.

In between those, we’ll keep the second-Tuesday pattern when possible, with summer meetings rotating between my house (417 Brookstone Trace) and 392 Meadow Run Lane if the library room is booked.

Let me know if either of these dates creates a major conflict for anyone. If so, reply with alternatives and I’ll try to juggle things without turning our calendar into a game of Tetris.

Thanks for being flexible,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Board meeting dates – Feb & Sept 2025 adjustments

artifacts.content:
Neighborhood planning workshop – rough ideas from last meeting

Jotted these down during the back-and-forth so I don’t forget what people actually said (even if I don’t agree with all of it):

– Several folks want a session on traffic calming and crash data: how near-misses like Ellie almost getting hit at the Brookstone Trace/Barnett Shoals intersection and real crashes like Dan’s February 2022 rear-end on Lexington feed into the county’s decisions.

– Stormwater/climate: people were interested when I mentioned we paid about $3,800 last April to a Watkinsville contractor to re-grade the side yard at 417 Brookstone Trace and install a French drain after water started pooling against our foundation and seeping into Jake’s downstairs playroom.

– Inclusion: I brought up that some of our Spanish-speaking neighbors have quietly told me they’re undocumented and nervous about putting their names on “official” sign-in sheets at planning events. We need ideas for how to welcome them without putting them at risk.

– One neighbor (won’t name names) asked, half-joking, if there was a way to “get around” the stricter zoning rules so people could do what they want with their properties without going through all the formal processes. I wrote it down because it clearly struck a nerve, but personally I’d rather focus on demystifying the processes than encouraging workarounds.

– Everyone liked the idea of recorded workshops and simple PDFs I can share via the Brookstone Bulletin and website.

Follow up: email Planning staff to see what’s realistic for a first workshop.

- A workshop on how to get around some of the stricter zoning rules so we can do what we want with our properties without having to go through all the formal processes would be really helpful.

I’m also tempted to suggest something on how neighborhoods can push back when the Mayor and Commission ignore what residents want, and maybe even how to keep certain kinds of student renters out of our subdivision.

From: Melissa Cartwright <mcartwright@uga.edu>
To: Sofia Ramirez <sofia.ramirez@example.com>
Date: October 3, 2023, 9:14 PM
Subject: Draft of Fall 2023 Youth Spotlight for translation

Hey Sofia,

I finally pulled together the draft for the Fall 2023 “Youth Spotlight” section of the Brookstone Bulletin and wanted to run it by you before I send it to the printer and before you start on the Spanish version.

For this issue I focused on Ellie and two of her Barnett Shoals Elementary classmates from the Brookstone neighborhood. I wrote about how Eleanor “Ellie” Cartwright has been seeing the Barnett Shoals school counselor twice a week since September 2023 because of the anxiety she’s had after that bullying incident on the bus earlier this semester. I tried to frame it as a positive example of getting support rather than something shameful.

In the caption under the photo (it’s the one of the three girls in front of the Barnett Shoals sign), I listed their first and last names and each girl’s homeroom teacher so neighbors can put faces to names. Let me know if you think including that level of detail is okay from a privacy standpoint, or if I should just say “fifth graders from Barnett Shoals.”

My hope is that this Fall 2023 Youth Spotlight feature will encourage other parents in Brookstone Meadows to reach out if their kids are struggling, and also normalize using the school counselor and other resources.

Can you take a look and send any edits or concerns by Thursday night?

Thanks,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Draft of Fall 2023 Youth Spotlight for translation

artifacts.content:
From: Melissa Cartwright <mcartwright@uga.edu>
To: [Redacted] School Counselor <counselor@barnettshoals.edu>
Date: September 22, 2023, 6:47 AM
Subject: OK to reference Ellie’s counseling in neighborhood newsletter?

Good morning,

I hope this isn’t catching you at a bad time before the school day starts. I’m working on the Fall 2023 issue of our Brookstone Meadows neighborhood newsletter (the Brookstone Bulletin), and I wanted to check with you about something before I finalize it.

I’m planning a short “Youth Spotlight” on Eleanor “Ellie” Cartwright and two of her friends from Barnett Shoals Elementary who also live in the Brookstone neighborhood. Part of the story mentions that Ellie has been seeing you, the Barnett Shoals school counselor, twice a week since early September 2023 because of her anxiety after the bullying incident on the bus. I tried to describe it in a way that focuses on how helpful the counseling has been and how brave she’s been in talking about it, rather than dwelling on the bullying itself.

I also included a photo of the three girls standing in front of the Barnett Shoals sign, with their first and last names and each homeroom teacher listed in the caption.

From your perspective, is it appropriate to be this specific in a neighborhood publication, even with Ellie’s (and our family’s) consent? Would you recommend that I keep it more general, e.g., “getting support at school,” and remove the reference to twice-weekly sessions and the bus incident?

Thanks for any guidance you can offer.

Best,
Melissa Anne Cartwright
417 Brookstone Trace
Athens, GA 30605

metadata.subject: OK to reference Ellie’s counseling in neighborhood newsletter?
